701	 * Abstract forward pass function for image-text-to-text or audio-text-to-text models.
702	 * @param {Object} self The model object.
703	 * @param {Object} params Additional parameters.
704	 * @param {Function} [params.encode_function] The function to encode the modality values.
705	 * @param {Function} [params.merge_function] The function to merge the modality features with the input embeddings.
706	 * @param {string} [params.modality_input_name] The modality input name.
707	 * @param {string} [params.modality_output_name] The modality output name.
708	 * @param {Tensor} [params.input_ids=null]
709	 * @param {Tensor} [params.attention_mask=null]
710	 * @param {Tensor} [params.position_ids=null]
711	 * @param {Tensor} [params.inputs_embeds=null]
712	 * @param {Tensor} [params.past_key_values=null]
713	 * @param {Object} [params.generation_config=null]
714	 * @param {Object} [params.logits_processor=null]
715	 * @returns {Promise<Tensor>} The model's output tensor
716	 * @private
717	 */
718	async function genericTextToTextForward(self, {
719	    // Generic parameters:
720	    encode_function,
721	    merge_function,
722	    modality_input_name,
723	    modality_output_name,
724	
725	    // Produced by the tokenizer/processor:
726	    input_ids = null,
727	    attention_mask = null,
728	
729	    // Used during generation:
730	    position_ids = null,
731	    inputs_embeds = null,
732	    past_key_values = null,
733	
734	    // Generic generation parameters
735	    generation_config = null,
736	    logits_processor = null,
737	
738	    // Additional parameters
739	    ...kwargs
740	}) {
741	    const modality_values = kwargs[modality_input_name];
742	    if (!inputs_embeds) {
743	        // 1. Extract the text embeddings.
744	        inputs_embeds = await self.encode_text({ input_ids, ...kwargs });
745	
746	        // 2. Possibly, merge text and modality values
747	        if (modality_values && input_ids.dims[1] !== 1) {
748	            const modality_features = await encode_function({
749	                // Pass the modality values under its expected key.
750	                // The caller knows whether this is audio or image.
751	                [modality_input_name]: modality_values,
752	                ...kwargs
753	            });
754	            ({ inputs_embeds, attention_mask } = merge_function({
755	                [modality_output_name]: modality_features,
756	                inputs_embeds,
757	                input_ids,
758	                attention_mask,
759	            }));
760	
761	        } else if (past_key_values && modality_values && input_ids.dims[1] === 1) {
762	            // This branch handles the cache case.
763	            const target_length = input_ids.dims[1]; // always 1
764	            const past_length = Object.values(past_key_values)[0].dims.at(-2);
765	
766	            attention_mask = cat([
767	                ones([input_ids.dims[0], past_length]),
768	                attention_mask.slice(null, [attention_mask.dims[1] - target_length, attention_mask.dims[1]]),
769	            ], 1);
770	        }
771	    }
772	
773	    if (!position_ids) {
774	
775	        if (self.config.model_type === 'qwen2_vl') {
776	            // Special case for qwen2_vl models
777	            // @ts-ignore
778	            const { image_grid_thw, video_grid_thw } = kwargs;
779	            [position_ids] = self.get_rope_index(input_ids, image_grid_thw, video_grid_thw, attention_mask)
780	        }
781	    }
782	
783	    // 3. Call the decoder forward using the updated inputs.
784	    const outputs = await decoderForward(self, {
785	        inputs_embeds,
786	        past_key_values,
787	        attention_mask,
788	        position_ids,
789	        generation_config,
790	        logits_processor,
791	    }, true);
792	    return outputs;
793	}

1 # The `pipeline` API 2 3 Just like the [transformers Python library](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers), Transformers.js provides users with a simple way to leverage the power of transformers. The `pipeline()` function is the easiest and fastest way to use a pretrained model for inference. 4 5 6 7 For the full list of available tasks/pipelines, check out [this table](#available-tasks). 8 9 10 11 12 ## The basics 13 14 Start by creating an instance of `pipeline()` and specifying a task you want to use it for. For example, to create a sentiment analysis pipeline, you can do: 15 16 ```javascript 17 import { pipeline } from '@huggingface/transformers'; 18 19 const classifier = await pipeline('sentiment-analysis'); 20 ``` 21 22 When running for the first time, the `pipeline` will download and cache the default pretrained model associated with the task. This can take a while, but subsequent calls will be much faster. 23 24 25 26 By default, models will be downloaded from the [Hugging Face Hub](https://huggingface.co/models) and stored in [browser cache](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Cache), but there are ways to specify custom models and cache locations. For more information see [here](./custom_usage). 27 28 29 30 You can now use the classifier on your target text by calling it as a function: 31 32 ```javascript 33 const result = await classifier('I love transformers!'); 34 // [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.9998}] 35 ``` 36 37 If you have multiple inputs, you can pass them as an array: 38 39 ```javascript 40 const result = await classifier(['I love transformers!', 'I hate transformers!']); 41 // [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.9998}, {'label': 'NEGATIVE', 'score': 0.9982}] 42 ``` 43 44 You can also specify a different model to use for the pipeline by passing it as the second argument to the `pipeline()` function. For example, to use a different model for sentiment analysis (like one trained to predict sentiment of a review as a number of stars between 1 and 5), you can do: 45 46 47 48 ```javascript 49 const reviewer = await pipeline('sentiment-analysis', 'Xenova/bert-base-multilingual-uncased-sentiment'); 50 51 const result = await reviewer('The Shawshank Redemption is a true masterpiece of cinema.'); 52 // [{label: '5 stars', score: 0.8167929649353027}] 53 ``` 54 55 Transformers.js supports loading any model hosted on the Hugging Face Hub, provided it has ONNX weights (located in a subfolder called `onnx`). For more information on how to convert your PyTorch, TensorFlow, or JAX model to ONNX, see the [conversion section](./custom_usage#convert-your-models-to-onnx). 56 57 The `pipeline()` function is a great way to quickly use a pretrained model for inference, as it takes care of all the preprocessing and postprocessing for you. For example, if you want to perform Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) using OpenAI's Whisper model, you can do: 58 59 60 ```javascript 61 // Create a pipeline for Automatic Speech Recognition 62 const transcriber = await pipeline('automatic-speech-recognition', 'Xenova/whisper-small.en'); 63 64 // Transcribe an audio file, loaded from a URL. 65 const result = await transcriber('https://huggingface.co/datasets/Narsil/asr_dummy/resolve/main/mlk.flac'); 66 // {text: ' I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed.'} 67 ``` 68 69 ## Pipeline options 70 71 ### Loading 72 73 We offer a variety of options to control how models are loaded from the Hugging Face Hub (or locally). 74 By default, when running in-browser, a *quantized* version of the model is used, which is smaller and faster, 75 but usually less accurate. To override this behaviour (i.e., use the unquantized model), you can use a custom 76 `PretrainedOptions` object as the third parameter to the `pipeline` function: 77 78 ```javascript 79 // Create a pipeline for feature extraction, using the full-precision model (fp32) 80 const pipe = await pipeline('feature-extraction', 'Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2', { 81 dtype: "fp32", 82 }); 83 ``` 84 Check out the section on [quantization](./guides/dtypes) to learn more. 85 86 You can also specify which revision of the model to use, by passing a `revision` parameter. 87 Since the Hugging Face Hub uses a git-based versioning system, you can use any valid git revision specifier (e.g., branch name or commit hash). 88 89 ```javascript 90 const transcriber = await pipeline('automatic-speech-recognition', 'Xenova/whisper-tiny.en', { 91 revision: 'output_attentions', 92 }); 93 ``` 94 95 For the full list of options, check out the [PretrainedOptions](./api/utils/hub#module_utils/hub..PretrainedOptions) documentation. 96 97 98 ### Running 99 Many pipelines have additional options that you can specify. For example, when using a model that does multilingual translation, you can specify the source and target languages like this: 100 101 102 ```javascript 103 // Create a pipeline for translation 104 const translator = await pipeline('translation', 'Xenova/nllb-200-distilled-600M'); 105 106 // Translate from English to Greek 107 const result = await translator('I like to walk my dog.', { 108 src_lang: 'eng_Latn', 109 tgt_lang: 'ell_Grek' 110 }); 111 // [ { translation_text: 'Μου αρέσει να περπατάω το σκυλί μου.' } ] 112 113 // Translate back to English 114 const result2 = await translator(result[0].translation_text, { 115 src_lang: 'ell_Grek', 116 tgt_lang: 'eng_Latn' 117 }); 118 // [ { translation_text: 'I like to walk my dog.' } ] 119 ``` 120 121 When using models that support auto-regressive generation, you can specify generation parameters like the number of new tokens, sampling methods, temperature, repetition penalty, and much more. For a full list of available parameters, see to the [GenerationConfig](./api/utils/generation#module_utils/generation.GenerationConfig) class. 122 123 For example, to generate a poem using `LaMini-Flan-T5-783M`, you can do: 124 125 126 127 ```javascript 128 // Create a pipeline for text2text-generation 129 const poet = await pipeline('text2text-generation', 'Xenova/LaMini-Flan-T5-783M'); 130 const result = await poet('Write me a love poem about cheese.', { 131 max_new_tokens: 200, 132 temperature: 0.9, 133 repetition_penalty: 2.0, 134 no_repeat_ngram_size: 3, 135 }); 136 ``` 137 138 Logging `result[0].generated_text` to the console gives: 139 140 ``` 141 Cheese, oh cheese! You're the perfect comfort food. 142 Your texture so smooth and creamy you can never get old. 143 With every bite it melts in your mouth like buttery delights 144 that make me feel right at home with this sweet treat of mine. 145 146 From classic to bold flavor combinations, 147 I love how versatile you are as an ingredient too? 148 Cheddar is my go-to for any occasion or mood; 149 It adds depth and richness without being overpowering its taste buds alone 150 ``` 151 152 ### Streaming 153 154 Some pipelines such as `text-generation` or `automatic-speech-recognition` support streaming output. This is achieved using the `TextStreamer` class. For example, when using a chat model like `Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B-Instruct`, you can specify a callback function that will be called with each generated token text (if unset, new tokens will be printed to the console). 155 156 ```js 157 import { pipeline, TextStreamer } from "@huggingface/transformers"; 158 159 // Create a text generation pipeline 160 const generator = await pipeline( 161 "text-generation", 162 "onnx-community/Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B-Instruct", 163 { dtype: "q4" }, 164 ); 165 166 // Define the list of messages 167 const messages = [ 168 { role: "system", content: "You are a helpful assistant." }, 169 { role: "user", content: "Write a quick sort algorithm." }, 170 ]; 171 172 // Create text streamer 173 const streamer = new TextStreamer(generator.tokenizer, { 174 skip_prompt: true, 175 // Optionally, do something with the text (e.g., write to a textbox) 176 // callback_function: (text) => { /* Do something with text */ }, 177 }) 178 179 // Generate a response 180 const result = await generator(messages, { max_new_tokens: 512, do_sample: false, streamer }); 181 ``` 182 183 Logging `result[0].generated_text` to the console gives: 184 185 186

189	Here's a simple implementation of the quick sort algorithm in Python:
190	```python
191	def quick_sort(arr):
192	    if len(arr) <= 1:
193	        return arr
194	    pivot = arr[len(arr) // 2]
195	    left = [x for x in arr if x < pivot]
196	    middle = [x for x in arr if x == pivot]
197	    right = [x for x in arr if x > pivot]
198	    return quick_sort(left) + middle + quick_sort(right)
199	# Example usage:
200	arr = [3, 6, 8, 10, 1, 2]
201	sorted_arr = quick_sort(arr)
202	print(sorted_arr)
203	```
204	### Explanation:
205	- **Base Case**: If the array has less than or equal to one element (i.e., `len(arr)` is less than or equal to `1`), it is already sorted and can be returned as is.
206	- **Pivot Selection**: The pivot is chosen as the middle element of the array.
207	- **Partitioning**: The array is partitioned into three parts: elements less than the pivot (`left`), elements equal to the pivot (`middle`), and elements greater than the pivot (`right`). These partitions are then recursively sorted.
208	- **Recursive Sorting**: The subarrays are sorted recursively using `quick_sort`.
209	This approach ensures that each recursive call reduces the problem size by half until it reaches a base case.
210
211
212 213 This streaming feature allows you to process the output as it is generated, rather than waiting for the entire output to be generated before processing it. 214 215 216 For more information on the available options for each pipeline, refer to the [API Reference](./api/pipelines). 217 If you would like more control over the inference process, you can use the [`AutoModel`](./api/models), [`AutoTokenizer`](./api/tokenizers), or [`AutoProcessor`](./api/processors) classes instead. 218 219 220 ## Available tasks 221 222 223 { 224 "path": "../snippets/5_supported-tasks.snippet" 225 } 226 227
